coredata.dc:title: Multicolor fluorescent probe for visual point-of-care detection of water via a smartphone
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Haibo
Li, Yongying
Lin, Bixia
Yu, Ying
Wang, Yumin
Zhang, Li
Cao, Yujuan
Guo, Manli
coredata.dc:description: The application of multicolor fluorescent probes in visual detection of targets has attracted considerable interest. Herein, a multicolor fluorescent probe (G/R-CDs) was fabricated by combining green carbon dots (G-CDs) with red carbon dots (R-CDs). As water content increased, the fluorescence of R-CDs at 683 nm was quenched significantly, resulting in the decrease of red fluorescence. To improve the visualization effect, G-CDs were introduced. The fluorescence peak of G-CDs was red shifted from 500 nm to 535 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was basically unchanged with addition of water. Therefore, G-CDs and R-CDs served as reference signal and response signal, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F 535/F 683) showed a good linear relationship with water content in the ranges of 3%-30% and 40%-60% with a low detection limit of 1.2%. More importantly, G-CDs also acted as a toner to enrich the fluorescence color of G/R-CDs. Under the toning of G-CDs, G/R-CDs presented a variety of fluorescent colors such as pink, orange, gray and green towards different water contents. Based on the rich color changes, the visual analysis was achieved by the naked eye. In addition, a smartphone was used to read out the RGB values for establishing the RGB method. The method was successfully applied for the detection of water in medical alcohol and commercial liquor, showing good practicability in visual point-of-care detection of water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor fluorescent probe
Point-of-care detection
Visualization
Water content

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual detection of water by RGB method
Construction of multicolor fluorescent probe G/R-CDs
Sensing mechanism of water by G/R-CDs
Visual detection of water by RGB method
Detection of water in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of water on the fluorescence of R-CDs, G-CDs and G/R-CDs. Fluorescence spectra of (A) R-CDs and (B) G-CDs with different contents of water; (C) Fluorescence spectra of G-CDs, R-CDs and G/R-CDs; (D) Fluorescence spectra of G/R-CDs with different contents of water. The volume ratio of G-CDs and R-CDs is 1:1.

Sensing Mechanism of water by G/R-CDs. UV–vis absorption spectra of (A) G-CDs and (B) R-CDs; (C) TEM images of G-CDs before (left) and after (right) adding water; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs; (E) TEM images of R-CDs before (left) and after (right) adding water; (F) Fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs. The water content is 50%.

Fluorescence spectra of G/R-CDs with different contents of water. The total volume of G/R-CDs is 200 µL, and the volume ratios of G-CDs to R-CDs are (A) 1:1, (B) 3:4, (C) 2:3 and (D) 1:2, respectively. The inset shows the corresponding photos under 365 nm UV lamp.

Selectivity of G/R-CDs probe. (A) Interference of organic compounds and (B) The corresponding photos under UV lamp; (C) Interference of ions and (D) The corresponding photos under UV lamp. Blank group is the single G/R-CDs. Control group is G/R-CDs detecting 30% water. The other groups are G/R-CDs detecting 30% water in the presence of 1% organic compounds or 100 µM ions. MT: Methanol; NPA: n-Propanol; IBA: Isobutanol; IAA: Isoamyl alcohol; EAC: Ethyl acetate; CP: Acetone; ACN: Acetonitrile; AH: Acetaldehyde; HAc: Acetic Acid; LA: Lactic acid.
The construction of a multicolor fluorescent probe G/R-CDs and the visual point-of-care detection of water.
Detection of water content in real samples.

head.ce:title: Multicolor fluorescent probe for visual point-of-care detection of water via a smartphone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors. No. 378 Waihuan West Road, University City, Guangzhou, Guangdong 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of multicolor fluorescent probes in visual detection of targets has attracted considerable interest. Herein, a multicolor fluorescent probe (G/R-CDs) was fabricated by combining green carbon dots (G-CDs) with red carbon dots (R-CDs). As water content increased, the fluorescence of R-CDs at 683 nm was quenched significantly, resulting in the decrease of red fluorescence. To improve the visualization effect, G-CDs were introduced. The fluorescence peak of G-CDs was red shifted from 500 nm to 535 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was basically unchanged with addition of water. Therefore, G-CDs and R-CDs served as reference signal and response signal, respectively. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F 535/F 683) showed a good linear relationship with water content in the ranges of 3%-30% and 40%-60% with a low detection limit of 1.2%. More importantly, G-CDs also acted as a toner to enrich the fluorescence color of G/R-CDs. Under the toning of G-CDs, G/R-CDs presented a variety of fluorescent colors such as pink, orange, gray and green towards different water contents. Based on the rich color changes, the visual analysis was achieved by the naked eye. In addition, a smartphone was used to read out the RGB values for establishing the RGB method. The method was successfully applied for the detection of water in medical alcohol and commercial liquor, showing good practicability in visual point-of-care detection of water.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is one of the most common substances. Daily life and production are inseparable from water. However, in some fields such as food processing, chemical production, biomedicine monitoring [1,2], water is generally considered as one of the most common impurities. The presence of water may cause hydrolysis of organic reagents and deterioration of product [3]. In the liquor industry, there is also strict stipulation on the water content [4]. Therefore, it is significant to establish a point-of-care detection method for water content. The traditional method for detecting water is Karl Fischer titration [5]. In addition, thermogravimetric analysis [6], electrochemical method [7], gas chromatography [8] and nuclear magnetic resonance [9] are also applied in the detection of water. Despite of many advantages of each method, they still confront some problems, such as the requirement for large equipment and professional operations, high detection cost and time-consuming procedures, which limit their application in point-of-care detection [10,11].

Due to the advantages of high sensitivity, simple operation and rapid detection, fluorescence method has been used in food safety [12,13], environmental monitoring [14,15] and life science [16,17]. In particular, fluorescence method possesses visualization effect which enjoys great popularity for analyte identifying and measuring [18]. When fluorescent probe detects varied concentrations of analyte, the fluorescence peak will differ in wavelengths or intensities, leading to the changes in fluorescence color. By judging from the fluorescence color changes, the visual quantitative analysis of analyte can be realized with the naked eye [19]. Since people’s naked eye is much more sensitive to the changes in different colors than a single color, the development of fluorescent probe with rich colors responding to the various concentration of analyte has become a research hotspot [20,21]. For example, Jiang et al. [22] used europium ion and cadmium telluride quantum dots to develop a broad-chromatic fluorescent probe for tetracycline. With the tetracycline addition, the fluorescence of probe changed from green to yellow and finally to red. However, the fluorescent probes currently used in water detection are mainly focused on the brightness variation of a single fluorescence color or transformation of two fluorescence colors [23]. For example, Yang et al. [24] designed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on copper nanoclusters and carbon dots. With the increase of water content, the orange fluorescence of copper nanoclusters decreased, while the blue fluorescence of carbon dots remained stable. The final fluorescence color of the probe changed from orange to blue.

In addition, it is known that making assessments with naked eye inevitably possesses the risk of personal error [25]. Smartphone provides a wonderful idea for excluding the individual difference to improve the accuracy of visual analysis [26]. Yu et al. [27] reported a smartphone platform to identify RGB values of fluorescence color of the detection system for visual and quantitative detection of Cd2+. The camera of smartphone was used to take photos of the changes of fluorescence color, then the color recognition application installed in smartphone read out the RGB values of photos. By using the relationship between RGB values and the concentrations of analyte, the visual detection was realized. Similarly, our research group [28] also reported smartphone for detecting antibiotic streptomycin. Streptomycin aptamer recognized streptomycin, and the excess aptamer hybridized with the complementary DNA to form double-stranded DNA (dsDNA). SYBR Green I combined with dsDNA and produced green fluorescence which increased with the decrease of streptomycin concentration. By using smartphone to take photos and analyze the RGB value, the relationship between G value and streptomycin concentration was used to establish digital image colorimetry for detecting streptomycin. However, since the method was based on the changes in brightness of single green fluorescence, the visualization effect by the naked eye was unsatisfactory.

Herein, a multicolor fluorescent probe (G/R-CDs) was constructed by integrating green carbon dots (G-CDs) with red carbon dots (R-CDs). As shown in Scheme 1 , G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method and solvothermal method respectively. With the increase of water content, the fluorescence peak of G-CDs moved from 500 nm to 535 nm with the fluorescence color changing from blue to green due to solvent effect, and the fluorescence intensity kept stable. While the presence of water caused aggregation of R-CDs, which quenched red fluorescence due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. By combining the two kinds of carbon dots, a serial of significant fluorescence color changes including pink, orange, gray and green emerged with the increase of water content. Based on the rich color variations, the visual semi-quantitative analysis was achieved through the naked eye. Furthermore, a portable smartphone was used to establish the RGB method for visual point-of-care detection of water. More importantly, compared with the ratiometric fluorescent probes reported before, the color variation range of G/R-CDs was enlarged. Therefore, the visualization effect was greatly improved.

m-Phenylenediamine (m-PD) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phytic acid (PA, 50%) was obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol was provided by Sun Chemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Taxus leaves were purchased from Traditional Chinese Medicine Yangshengtang (Yongkang, China). Medical alcohol and liquor were obtained from Sunshine Life Supermarket (Guangzhou, China). The ultrapure water was obtained by using Milli-Q water purification system (Billerica, MA, USA).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by using a UV-2700 ultraviolet spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra assays were performed with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) under the voltage of 500 V. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on a FLS1000 transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by using a JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The UV lamp (365 nm) was obtained from Yuhua Instrument Co., ltd (Gongyi, China). All photos were taken with Huawei Honor 9 smartphone. The color recognition application was developed by Xiyi Technology.

G-CDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method [29] with some modifications. Briefly, 0.1080 g of m-PD and 4.0 mL of 50% PA were mixed with 6.0 mL of ultrapure water under stirring. The mixture was sealed into a 50 mL of teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 degreesC for 12 h. After naturally cooling, the resulting dark green solution was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 10 min. Then, the supernatant was filtered by a 0.22 μm PES (polyether sulfone) membrane and dried at 60 ℃ under vacuum overnight. Finally, G-CDs were re-dispersed in anhydrous ethanol for subsequent experiments. To investigate the quantum yield, quinine sulfate was used as the reference standard.

R-CDs were prepared according to solvothermal method [30] by using ethanol instead of acetone as the synthetic solvent. Firstly, dry taxus leaves were smashed into powder by a micromill, then 1.0 g of powder was added with 10 mL of anhydrous ethanol. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL of teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 120 degreesC for 5 h. After cooling naturally, the dark green solution was centrifuged, filtered and dried following the same procedure as G-CDs. The prepared R-CDs were re-dispersed in anhydrous ethanol for subsequent experiments. To investigate the quantum yield, quinine sulfate was used as the reference standard. For the preparation of fluorescent probe G/R-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs were mixed according to the volume ratio of 1:2.

In a series of colorimetric tubes, 200 µL of G/R-CDs solution was added. Subsequently, different volumes of ultrapure water (0, 60, 100, 200, 300, 400, 600, 800, 900, 1000, 1100 and 1200 µL) were separately added into the above G/R-CDs solution and diluted to 2.0 mL with anhydrous ethanol. The fluorescence spectrum of each solution was measured under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. By using the relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio at 535 nm and 683 nm and water content (v/v, %), the calibration curve was established to detect water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of water by RGB method
ce:section.ce:para: The series of solutions prepared in Section 2.3 were transferred into cuvettes, and illuminated with a 365 nm UV lamp. Then, a smartphone was used to take photos of each solution, and the color recognition application obtained the RGB value of each photo. By using the relationship between the ratio of green channel and red channel and water content (v/v, %), the visual point-of-care detection method of water was established.

For the detection of real samples, 45 degree Beidacang liquor, 52 degree Red star erguotou, 56 degree Niulanshan erguotou, and 75% medical alcohol were chosen. The samples were filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane before detection. In a series of colorimetric tubes, 200 µL of G/R-CDs solution with G-CDs and R-CDs in the volume ratio of 1:2 was added. Then, 1800 μL of real samples were added respectively. The solutions were mixed well, then the fluorescence spectra and RGB values were measured according to the same procedure for calibration curve above. Finally, the water content was calculated from the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of multicolor fluorescent probe G/R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence spectrum of R-CDs with quantum yield of 2.7% exhibited a strong and sharp emission peak at 683 nm under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The fluorescence intensity at 683 nm decreased with the increase of water content. When the water content was 70%, the fluorescence quenching efficiency reached maximum. Meanwhile, the inset in Fig. 1A showed the fluorescence color of R-CDs changed from red to colourless with increasing water content under UV lamp. However, in Figure S1, when the same volume of ethanol was added instead of water, the red fluorescence of R-CDs did not change significantly. The results indicated that R-CDs were responsive to water. In Fig. 1B, G-CDs with quantum yield of 7.9% displayed a wide emission peak at 500 nm. When 5% water was present, the fluorescence peak had a significant red shift and became sharp. However, with the continuous increase of water content, the location and intensity of the fluorescence peak remained basically unchanged. The inset in Fig. 1B showed the fluorescence color changed from blue to green under UV lamp. However, in Figure S2, when the same volume of ethanol was added instead of water, the change of blue fluorescence was not obvious. The above results illustrated that G-CDs were responsive to water at low content (less than5%), suggesting that G-CDs were suitable for the detection of water content. However, when water content was more than 5%, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was not affected by water content, which indicated G-CDs could also acted as a reference signal.

In order to design a multicolor fluorescent probe to improve the visual effect of detection, a dual-emission fluorescent probe (G/R-CDs) was constructed by combining R-CDs and G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, G-CDs and R-CDs had an emission peak at 500 nm and 683 nm under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm, respectively. When G-CDs and R-CDs combined to form G/R-CDs, the two fluorescence peaks still maintained at 500 nm and 683 nm. The inset showed R-CDs, G-CDs and G/R-CDs emitted strong red, blue and pink fluorescence under the UV lamp, respectively. The visual effect of detecting water by G/R-CDs was investigated in Fig. 1D. When the water content increased, the fluorescence peak of G/R-CDs at 500 nm red-shifted to 535 nm firstly, and remained unchanged afterwards, while the fluorescence intensity at 683 nm decreased continually. From the photos under the UV lamp, it was amazing to find that G/R-CDs exhibited multicolor such as pink, orange, gray and green with the increase of water content. Therefore, the combined fluorescent probe G/R-CDs had multicolor fluorescence evolution responding to the water content, and could be applied for the visual detection of water content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of water by G/R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the sensing mechanism of water by G/R-CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra, transmission electron microscopy and fluorescence lifetime were measured. In Fig. 2 A and 2B, G-CDs and R-CDs showed absorption peaks at 393 nm and 408 nm, respectively. However, the addition of water had little influence on the UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs. Similarly, the absorption spectrum of G/R-CDs kept unchanged with water (Figure S3), indicating that no new substance was produced. Besides, since water had no absorption in the UV–visible region, the mechanisms of fluorescence resonance energy transfer and inner-filter effect were excluded. In order to observe the morphology of G-CDs, TEM test was carried out before and after adding water. In Fig. 2C, G-CDs exhibited good dispersion with an average particle size of 6.7 nm. After the addition of water, G-CDs still kept good dispersion with little change in particle size. For many conjugated aromatic compounds, π ∼ π* transitions occurred when they were excited, and the excited state of electron had greater polarity than the ground state. As the solvent was changed from ethanol to water, the polarity of solvent increased, producing a more favorable effect on the stability of the excited state than the ground state. As a result, the fluorescence peak was red shifted with the increased polarity of solvent [31,32]. Similarly, since G-CDs had conjugated benzene rings, the presence of water increased the polarity of solvent, causing the red shift of fluorescence peak of G-CDs. Therefore, the effect of water on G-CDs was attributed to solvent effect. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) was a common method for judging the solvent effect [33]. The presence of solvent effect was often accompanied by changes in the fluorescence lifetime. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetimes of G-CDs were tested before and after adding water. In Fig. 2D, with the addition of water, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs decreased from 5.75 ns to 3.69 ns, which further proved the solvent effect of water on G-CDs.

In addition, the morphology of R-CDs before and after adding water was also tested by TEM. In Fig. 2E, R-CDs were monodispersed and uniform with an average particle size of 3.5 nm. However, R-CDs aggregated when water was added, and the diameters of the aggregated R-CDs increased to about 16.9 nm. The TEM results proved that the fluorescence quenching of water on R-CDs was induced by aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect [30]. Subsequently, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs was explored in Fig. 2F. The fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs decreased from 8.87 ns to 6.47 ns after adding water, suggesting the dynamic quenching mechanism of water on R-CDs [34]. In summary, the sensing mechanism of G-CDs was solvent effect, while that of R-CDs included aggregation-caused quenching and dynamic quenching. The synergistic action of the above sensing mechanisms enabled the fluorescent probe G/R-CDs to show rich fluorescence colors responding to water, which was beneficial to the visual detection of water by the naked eye.

In order to obtain the optimal visual effect, the total volume and volume ratio of G-CDs and R-CDs were investigated in Figure S4, S5 and Fig. 3 . The results showed the increased total volume of G-CDs and R-CDs avoided the appearance of light colors for detecting water, which was more conducive to the visualization effect. Further, when the total volume of G-CDs and R-CDs was constant, the initial color changed from gray to pink as the proportion of R-CDs increased. Meanwhile, the water content for color mutation also changed. Taking Figure S5 as an example, when the volume ratio of G-CDs and R-CDs was 3:4, the color mutated from orange to gray with the water content changing from 10% to 15%. When the ratio was 2:3, the color mutated corresponding to 15% to 20% water content. When the ratio decreased to 1:2, the color mutated corresponding to 30% to 35% water content. To sum up, by adjusting the total volume and proportion of G-CDs and R-CDs, the fluorescence color of G/R-CDs changed among pink, orange, gray, and green. However, the optimum amount and proportion of G-CDs and R-CDs depended on the specific circumstances of actual samples. Herein, a total volume of 200 µL and a volume ratio of 1:2 were selected for subsequent experiments.

In addition, the optimal excitation wavelength of G/R-CDs and the response time to water were investigated. In Figure S6, there was an overlap at 390 nm between the excitation spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs. Therefore, 390 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength for G/R-CDs. In Figure S7, when water was added, the fluorescence ratio F 535/F 683 of G/R-CDs increased instantly and tended to be stable within 30 s, indicating that the response time of G/R-CDs to water was very short and G/R-CDs were suitable for point-of-care detection of water.

Under the optimum detection conditions, the linear relationship and limit of detection (LOD) were investigated for fluorescence detection of water. In Figure S8A, the emission intensity at 535 nm of G/R-CDs changed little with increasing water contents, whereas that at 683 nm decreased obviously. The ratio of fluorescence intensities at 535 nm and 683 nm displayed two-stage linearity with water content (Figure S8B). Figure S8C and S8D showed the linear equations were F 535/F 683 = 0.0028C + 0.5102 (C: %, v/v, R 2 = 0.998) in the range of 3%-30%, and F 535/F 683 = 0.1655C-5.695 (C: %, v/v, R 2 = 0.998) in the range of 40%-60%. The limit of detection was calculated as 1.2% (3σ/S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of water by RGB method
ce:section.ce:para: Under a portable UV lamp, a smartphone was used to take photos of G/R-CDs with different contents of water. As shown in Figure S9A, with the water content increasing from 0 to 85%, the fluorescence of G/R-CDs showed rich color changes including pink, orange, gray and green. However, when single R-CDs were used, the red fluorescence of R-CDs changed little with addition of less than 40% water. When water content was in the range of 40%∼85%, the red fluorescence of R-CDs gradually weakened and disappeared at last (Figure S9B). The results indicated that the introduction of G-CDs improved the sensitivity for visual detection of water. Furthermore, since R-CDs only showed fading in red color, the visualization effect was poor. While G/R-CDs exhibited colorful fluorescence to varied water contents, thereby, the visualization effect by the naked eye was significantly improved.

Subsequently, the color recognition application installed on smartphone was used for color recognition of the photos, and output of RGB values. As shown in Figure S10, when water content was in the range of 0–25%, 25%-50% and 50%-80%, the ratio of green channel and red channel had a good linear relationship with water content. The linear equations were y1 = 0.0084C + 0.689, y2 = 0.0033C + 0.817 and y3 = 0.0203C-0.0324 (C: %, v/v; R1
2 = 0.982, R2
2 = 0.999 and R3
2 = 0.998). The LOD was calculated as 1.3% (3σ/S).

The established method was compared with other reported fluorescence methods for detecting water in Table S1. The material of probe, linear range, limit of detection, fluorescence color variation and visual detection were compared. Although the developed method had a higher LOD, the linear range was wider. In addition, the fluorescence color evolution of the probes reported before was limited within a single color or between two colors. In this work, because the blue fluorescence of G-CDs turned green when water was added, the fluorescence color evolution of G/R-CDs presented multiple colors such as pink, orange, gray and green with the increase of water, which significantly increased the color variation. Thus the visualization effect based on G/R-CDs was better. Moreover, the RGB method based on G/R-CDs was established successfully for visual detection of water content, which excluded the individual difference to improve the accuracy of visual detection. The operation was simple and fast without professional equipment, thus it was suitable for point-of-care detection of water.

The stability of G/R-CDs was explored. In Figure S11A, under continuous irradiation, the fluorescence intensity ratio of G/R-CDs at 535 nm and 683 nm remained stable within 3600 s, indicating that G/R-CDs had excellent stability against photobleaching. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs (Figure S11B), R-CDs (Figure S11C) and G/R-CDs (Figure S11D) stored at room temperature for 25 days were measured respectively. The results showed that the fluorescence intensities of G-CDs, R-CDs and G/R-CDs after 25 days had a variation of less than 5% relative to the original intensities, indicating the good stability. The influence of pH on G/R-CDs was also investigated in Figure S12. When pH was in the range of 2–8, the influence on F 535/F 683 was not obvious.

Except for ethanol and water, there may be a small amount (less than 1%) of other organic compounds and ions (less than 100 µM) in liquor. In order to explore the interference of various organic compounds and ions on the detection of water content, the interference experiment was carried out in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4A, when 1% organic compounds existed in the detection system, the F 535/F 683 value changed less than 10%. In Fig. 4B, the presence of 1% organic compounds had little influence on the fluorescence color compared with the control group. This indicated that other organic compounds had little interference on the visual detection of water by G/R-CDs.

Various ions such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, F-, Cl-, S2-, PO4 3-, NO3 –, SO4 2- and CO3 2– with a final concentration of 100 µM were selected for investigation. In Fig. 4C and S13, when various ions existed, the F 535/F 683 value was close to that of the control group except the addition of copper ion. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4D and the inset in Figure S13, only the sample in presence of copper ion showed obviously different color, while the samples with other ions exhibited the same color as the control group. Because L-cysteine was able to form complex with copper ion [35], in order to eliminate the interference of copper ion, L-cysteine was added to combine with copper ion before detection. The results in Figure S13 showed the addition of L-cysteine effectively eliminated the interference of copper ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the practice application of G/R-CDs in the detection of real samples, 75% medical alcohol and commercial liquor (45 degree Beidacang liquor, 52 degree Red star erguotou and 56 degree Niulanshan erguotou) were detected. The experimental results were shown in Table 1 . The detection results of 75% medical alcohol and 56 degree liquor samples measured by RGB method were slightly higher or lower than the label values. This was because the water contents were low, and the fluorescence color changes of the two samples were not obvious. However, the results of other samples measured by RGB method and fluorescence method were consistent with the label values, which proved that the developed methods were accurate and reliable. In particular, no any professional equipment but a portable ultraviolet lamp and a smartphone were needed in the RGB method. The method was simple and quick, showing great practicability for the point-of-care detection of water in real samples.

A simple and visual RGB method was developed for detection of water based on a novel multicolor fluorescent probe G/R-CDs. When water content increased, the fluorescence wavelength of G-CDs red-shifted and then kept unchanged, whereas the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased continually, accompanying with rich fluorescence color changes such as pink, orange, gray and green. Compared with the reported dual-emission probes for water detection, G/R-CDs showed a wider variation range of fluorescence color, which acquired better visualization effect. Afterwards, combined with the color recognition application of smartphone, RGB method was established by simply reading out the RGB values of fluorescence color. The introduction of RGB method based on smartphone eliminated the personal error in vision, and realized the on-site visual detection of water. Moreover, the method was successfully applied for the determination of water in real samples such as medical alcohol and commercial liquor, showing great practicability. It provided a solution for the construction of a visual point-of-care detection method in the field of analytical chemistry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575043, 22004039, 52070080); Fund of Department of Education of Guangdong Province (No. 2020KTSCX033); Platform Construction Project of Guangzhou Science Technology and Innovation Commission (No. 15180001); Science and Technology Projects (Basic and Applied Basic Research) in Guangzhou (No.202102080612).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107221.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
